name: Outsider App Fixes and Improvements
description: Focused execution project for bringing the outsider experience up to date with tracker features, permission-safe sharing, and mobile-ready regressions.
content:
Objective
Bring the outsider experience up to date with the tracker feature set while preserving read-only access, privacy boundaries, and mobile usability.

Investigation summary

* The outsider app already has dedicated Overview, Telemetry, Goals, and Comms surfaces.
* The app currently builds and lints cleanly, so this is mainly a product execution and integration gap rather than a broken baseline.
* Outsider data loading in src/App.jsx currently relies on tracker_connections plus daily_entries history.
* The repo planning doc still marks appointments, period sharing, permission-safe policy updates, and outsider summary expansion as unfinished.
* The highest risk area is privacy drift: newer tracker features can be added without outsider policies, toggles, and UI states staying aligned.

Execution plan

1. Add backend policies and permission-safe access for appointments and period data.
2. Refactor outsider data loading into a normalized shared summary model that combines daily entries, todos, appointments, and period signals.
3. Expand the outsider Overview page with summary-level todo, appointments, and period blocks.
4. Expand the outsider Telemetry/Data page with read-only planning sections and a shared agenda if it improves scanability.
5. Tighten outsider support shortcuts and visibility rules so they respect per-connection permissions end to end.
6. Run regression QA across sharing combinations, privacy rules, existing charts/support flows, and mobile layouts.

Definition of done

* Outsider pages expose only approved summary-level data.
* Disabled permissions remove related outsider summaries, details, and nudges.
* Period data stays conservative, privacy-safe, and never exposes private notes.
* Existing outsider charts and support flows still work after the new data sources are added.
* Mobile layouts remain usable across the expanded outsider surfaces.

name: To-Do Deepening
description: Complete the next wave of task functionality with better scheduling, status handling, and a stronger data model.

name: Period Tracker Deepening
description: Expand period tracking with richer daily detail, safer summaries, and a faster active-cycle logging flow.

name: Appointments Deepening
description: Make appointments and reminders more useful without turning the app into a heavy calendar.

name: Navigation Cleanup
description: Finish the tracker information architecture shift toward a calmer Log hub and a stronger overview home surface.

name: Outsider Shared Planning
description: Bring outsider permissions, summaries, and support flows up to date with shared To-Do, Period, Appointments, and agenda data.

name: External Calendar Sync Plan
description: Optional external calendar sync after the in-app calendar is stable, with Google Calendar first and Apple-friendly support later.
content:
# External Calendar Sync Plan

This project recreates the external calendar sync planning section from docs/tracking-expansion-plan.md.

After the in-app calendar is stable, add optional sync with outside calendar systems so users can see tracker items in tools they already use.

Recommended rollout:

* finish the in-app calendar first
* add Google Calendar sync before Apple-specific sync
* support Apple Calendar through export or subscription before attempting deeper iCloud-specific behavior

Recommended Version 1 scope:

* app-to-calendar sync for appointments and reminders
* optional app-to-calendar sync for dated to-dos
* no write-back from external calendars into the app yet

Why this order:

* Google Calendar has a clearer and more developer-friendly sync path
* Apple Calendar support is easier through standard calendar formats than through deep direct iCloud integration
* the internal calendar needs stable event shapes before outside sync becomes safe and predictable

Execution phases:

1. Define the sync model
2. Prepare the data model
3. Build Google Calendar connection
4. Add app-to-Google sync rules
5. Add Apple-friendly support
6. Add controls and safeguards
7. Test and release carefully

Technical direction:
Build on top of the shared normalized event layer used by the in-app calendar. External calendars should connect to that normalized event layer rather than having each tracker type talk to Google or Apple separately.

name: Tracker Stabilization
description: Short-term stabilization work for tracker and outsider product quality, including privacy fixes, mobile responsiveness, UI cleanup, navigation cleanup, test coverage, and architecture risk reduction.

name: Calendar Visibility
description: Plan for making calendar events easier to understand across the in-app calendar and Google Calendar, starting with strong in-app visibility before deeper sync.

name: Calendar Feature
description: Create and polish the Calendar sync between in app and google/any others
